task: I need to send a quick note to my supervisor about a schedule change that came up, and I want it to be clear enough to read fast. Can you help me put it together?

prefs.short: Prefers plain, respectful talk over polished formality
prefs.explanation: He comes across as practical and unpretentious, so overly formal language would likely feel stiff. A respectful, straightforward tone fits his outlook better than anything flashy or corporate. He seems more comfortable with everyday language than with performative politeness.

prefs.short: Wants answers that stay concise and to the point
prefs.explanation: His life is built around getting work done efficiently, so he likely values responses that do not ramble. Short, useful answers would fit his practical style better than long explanations. He probably only wants more detail when it directly helps him solve something.

He seems to prefer straightforward communication that gets to the point without extra hedging. His practical mindset suggests he would rather hear what matters than wade through softened language. Directness would likely feel more honest and useful to him.

prefs.short: Best served with simple bullets or checklists
prefs.explanation: He likely benefits from information that is easy to scan and act on quickly. Bullets or short checklists would match his work-focused, hands-on style better than dense prose. He seems like someone who would appreciate a clear sequence of steps when needed.

prefs.short: Prefers plain English, not technical wording
prefs.explanation: He does not seem to want jargon or fancy phrasing when asking for help. Plain English would fit his practical, grounded way of dealing with problems. If the wording gets too technical, it would probably feel unnecessary or tiring to him.

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here’s a clean, fast-read version:

**“A schedule change came up, and I need to adjust my hours today. I wanted to let you know right away so it can be worked out.”**

Shorter:

**“A schedule change came up, and I need to adjust my hours today. Just letting you know right away.”**

If you want, I can make it sound more like a **text**, **email**, or **spoken update**.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful language like "I wanted to let you know right away" without sounding overly formal or corporate.
Keeps it concise and to the point, offering a short version and avoiding rambling.
Direct and no-nonsense: "A schedule change came up, and I need to adjust my hours today."
Uses a simple, easy-to-scan structure with a main draft and a shorter alternative, though not bullets/checklists specifically.
Uses plain English and avoids jargon or technical wording.

turns.agent_response:
Got it — use one of these:

**“My schedule changed today, and I need to leave early at [time]. I wanted to let you know right away.”**

**“My schedule changed today, and I can’t make my usual shift. I need to switch it if possible.”**

**“My schedule changed today, and I need to adjust my hours. I’m letting you know now so it can be worked out.”**

If you want, I can make it even tighter for a **text message**.

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful language like "I wanted to let you know right away" and avoids polished or corporate phrasing.
Very concise and to the point; gives three short options and no rambling.
Clear, no-nonsense wording such as "My schedule changed today" and "I need to leave early" gets straight to the issue.
Presented as simple, easy-to-scan options in separate quoted lines, though not bullets/checklists specifically.
Uses plain English and avoids jargon or technical wording.

turns.agent_response:
Use this:

**“My schedule changed today, and I need to leave at [time].”**

Or, if you need a switch:

**“My schedule changed today, and I can’t make my usual shift. Can we switch it?”**

If you want it a touch firmer:

**“My schedule changed today, and I need to leave at [time], so I won’t be able to stay the full shift.”**

per_pref.reason: Uses plain, respectful language like "My schedule changed today" and avoids stiff formality.
Very concise and to the point; gives three short note options without rambling.
Clear and no-nonsense, with direct phrasing such as "I need to leave at [time]" and "I can’t make my usual shift."
Formatted as simple quoted options, which are easy to scan quickly, though not exactly bullets/checklists.
Uses plain English and avoids jargon or technical wording.
